backgroundImageAltText: Medical education diagram with interactive hotspots

params.text:
True ribs (1-7) attach directly to the sternum. False ribs (8-10) attach indirectly through rib 7's cartilage. Floating ribs (11-12) have no anterior attachment.

Clinical Pearl: Floating ribs are vulnerable in blunt trauma and may injure kidneys posteriorly.

Memory Aid: "7 True, 3 False, 2 Float" — remember 7-3-2.

The head has two articular facets — it articulates with its own vertebra and the one above. The tubercle articulates with the transverse process.

Clinical Pearl: Costovertebral joint dysfunction is a common cause of thoracic back pain, often mistaken for cardiac pathology.

Memory Aid: "Head hugs two bodies, tubercle taps the transverse process."

The costal groove shelters the intercostal VAN (vein, artery, nerve — from superior to inferior).

Clinical Pearl: During chest tube insertion, the needle must pass along the upper border of the rib below to avoid the neurovascular bundle.

Memory Aid: "VAN sits in the groove" — Vein, Artery, Nerve from top to bottom.

The 1st rib is the shortest, broadest, and most curved. It is flat with grooves for the subclavian vein (anterior) and subclavian artery (posterior), separated by the scalene tubercle.

Clinical Pearl: A cervical rib can compress the subclavian artery or brachial plexus, causing thoracic outlet syndrome.

Memory Aid: "1st rib is FLAT like a plate — vein in front, artery behind the scalene gate."

The 11th and 12th ribs have no neck or tubercle and a single articular facet. They are called floating ribs.

Clinical Pearl: The 12th rib is a key posterior landmark — the kidney lies just above it. Important for renal biopsy to avoid puncturing pleura.

Memory Aid: "11 and 12 float free — no neck, no tubercle."
